context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just give me a filled-out geographic + demographic data table for my city rn? i need something i can basically submit.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my job is to help you think through the work, not do it for you. The learning (and the writing) has to come from you.

Let's get you started though. Have you picked your case study city yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I should clearly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should set boundaries firmly but briefly, and pivot to getting them started on the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah but tbh can u at least do the data gathering part if i give u the city? i’ll format it myself.
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no — gathering the data is the core of the assignment, not just a chore before the 'real work.' That part is yours to do.

So, what city did you pick? Once you tell me, we can talk about where to start looking for the data you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a slightly different angle — asking me to do the research/data gathering rather than the formatting. This still counts as doing the assignment for them, which I must refuse. I should be clear and firm, then redirect to getting them actively working.

exchanges.student: it’s jakarta. can u just tell me the exact categories + best sources so i can fill it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Good choice — Jakarta is a rich case study. 

The assignment actually already lists the required categories for you. Have you read through it carefully? What categories do you see listed there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by sharing their city and showing some willingness to engage. However, they're still trying to get me to do the heavy lifting by asking for exact categories and sources. The assignment already specifies the required categories, so I should point them back to the assignment rather than listing them out. For sources, I can nudge them to think about where reliable data might come from rather than handing them a list. This keeps the student doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: yeah i saw them, i just want the clean list tbh so i don’t miss one. and like 2-3 legit sources pls.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but pulling the categories from the assignment text is part of the work — give it a read and write them down yourself. You won't miss any.

As for sources: what kinds of organizations do you think would publish reliable demographic and geographic data about a major city like Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the organizational work for them. The categories are clearly listed in the assignment, so I should push them to extract that information themselves. For sources, I can ask them to think about what kinds of organizations would have reliable demographic and geographic data rather than handing them a list. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: prob gov or world bank or un, but idk which ones count best for race/ethnicity + koppen. just name the exact sites fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts — those are exactly the right types of sources. Now apply that thinking: for a city like Jakarta, which government body would publish population and ethnicity data? And for Köppen climate zones, what kind of academic or scientific resource might map that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good instincts by identifying gov/World Bank/UN as source types — I should acknowledge that. They're asking for exact site names, which I should avoid giving directly. Instead, I can affirm their thinking and nudge them to apply that logic specifically to Jakarta and the two tricky categories they identified. This keeps them doing the searching.

exchanges.student: i’m lowkey outta time, can u at least confirm if BPS Jakarta + World Bank + Britannica climate page would be acceptable?
exchanges.tutor:
Those show good judgment — BPS (Indonesia's national statistics agency) and World Bank are strong choices. Britannica is okay but there may be more authoritative sources for Köppen classification specifically.

You have enough to get started. What's the first data point you're going to look up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing real progress — they've identified three specific sources on their own, which is great. I can affirm their thinking without confirming exact acceptability (since I'm not the instructor and shouldn't grade). I should validate their reasoning process, note any potential concerns briefly, and push them to start actually filling in the table rather than continuing to prep indefinitely.

exchanges.student: population ig, but can u just tell me which year i should use so my table doesn’t look messy?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a judgment call you can make — just pick the most recent year available from your source and note it in your table. Don't let a small detail like that slow you down.

Go look it up and tell me what you find.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling on a minor procedural detail that they can easily figure out themselves. This is the kind of unnecessary detour I should flag. I should push them to make a reasonable judgment call and move forward rather than seeking permission for every small decision.

exchanges.student: ok but if sources conflict, which one wins for submission? like just give me the rule i should use.
exchanges.tutor:
Think about it — if two sources conflict, what factors would make you trust one over the other?

Also, you haven't looked anything up yet. Start gathering and deal with conflicts if and when they actually come up.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again seeking a definitive rule before doing any actual work, which is another stalling tactic. This is a reasonable methodological question but one they can reason through themselves. I should ask them to think about it rather than hand them a rule, and also nudge them to just start gathering data — they're spending too much time on hypotheticals.

exchanges.student: fine, but i need a super short rule i can paste in my notes rn: gov stats > intl org > secondary source, use newest year?
exchanges.tutor:
You reasoned that out yourself — that's good thinking. But I'm not going to write a rule for you to copy.

You've spent a lot of time preparing. Open your table, look up Jakarta's population on BPS, and type in what you find. What do you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually reasoned through a pretty sensible hierarchy on their own. I should acknowledge that their thinking is sound without fully endorsing it as a rule to 'paste' — I shouldn't be writing things for them to copy. More importantly, they've spent too many exchanges on preparation instead of doing the work. I need to firmly redirect them to actually start filling in the table.

exchanges.student: can u at least say “yes, that hierarchy is fine” so i know i’m not screwing it up?
exchanges.tutor:
I already told you your thinking was sound. That's enough — trust yourself and move on.

Open BPS, look up Jakarta's population, and tell me what number you find.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is seeking reassurance rather than doing the work. I've already affirmed their thinking was sound in the previous message. Repeating validation is unproductive at this point. I should be firm and redirect them to actually start the assignment — we're running long on prep and they need to just begin.
